content.text: The Case Against Over-Agentification
Agents are powerful, but they are not free. Every agentic layer adds
latency, cost, unpredictability, and debugging complexity

. Wrapping a simple task in an LLM-driven agent loop when a plain function would do is one of the most common engineering mistakes in AI projects. This topic gives you the tools to recognise when an agent is the wrong choice.

The question is not 'Could an agent do this?' — it almost always could. The question is 'Does the task justify the overhead?'. Agents earn their keep when the work requires dynamic decisions, open-ended tool use, or handling uncertainty that cannot be encoded into deterministic rules.

attrs.alt: Decision tree: use an agent vs. a simpler tool based on reasoning requirements, cost, and determinism
attrs.title: When to Use an Agent vs. a Simpler Tool

content.text: Clear Signs You Do Not Need an Agent
The task is fully deterministic.
If the same input always produces the same output through a fixed sequence of steps, a script or function is correct. Agents introduce non-determinism for no gain here.
You can enumerate all execution paths.
If you can draw the complete flowchart of every possible outcome before writing the code, a workflow engine or state machine is the right tool.
The task is structured data transformation.
Parsing a CSV, transforming JSON, calling a REST API, or running a SQL query does not require natural-language reasoning. Use the appropriate libraries directly.
Latency requirements are strict.
A single LLM call typically takes 1–10 seconds. A multi-step agent loop can take minutes. For real-time systems (sub-100ms), agents cannot meet the requirement.
Volume is high and cost matters.
Processing millions of records with an LLM agent loop is orders of magnitude more expensive than a rule-based system. Evaluate cost before architecture.
Auditability and reproducibility are required.
Regulated environments (finance, healthcare) often require deterministic, reproducible outputs. Agent non-determinism is a compliance problem.
The Agent vs. Alternative Decision Table
Situation
Better choice
Why not an agent
Nightly ETL pipeline
Airflow / dbt DAG
Steps are fixed; agent adds cost and fragility
Form validation
Schema validation library
Deterministic rules; sub-ms latency needed
Fraud scoring (fixed features)
Traditional ML classifier
10ms SLA; LLM 100x slower and 1000x costlier
Sending a scheduled email
Cron job + email API
No reasoning required; agent is pure overhead
API routing (known endpoints)
Router / load balancer
Enumerable conditions; rule-based is testable
Anti-Pattern 1: Wrapping a Function in an Agent
A common mistake is treating the agent as a universal adapter. The code below illustrates the problem:

# BAD: agent used to add two numbers
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

def add_with_agent(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model='claude-haiku-4-5-20251001',
        max_tokens=50,
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': f'What is {a} + {b}?'}]
    )
    return int(response.content[0].text.strip())

# This call costs ~$0.0002 per invocation, takes ~1 second,
# and can hallucinate the answer.
result = add_with_agent(3, 4)

# GOOD: just use Python
result = 3 + 4  # free, 0ms, always correct

The LLM adds nothing here — Python does arithmetic better, faster, and for free. This pattern scales up: use agents only when the work genuinely requires language understanding or open-ended tool use.
Anti-Pattern 2: Agent for Structured Data Extraction
When data has a predictable schema, a regex, XPath, or schema validator is the right tool. An agent is justified only when the input is unstructured or natural language.

import re
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class OrderEvent:
    order_id: str
    amount: float
    status: str

# BAD: using an LLM to parse structured logs
def parse_with_agent(log_line: str) -> OrderEvent:
    # 1s latency, $0.001 per call, non-deterministic
    ...

# GOOD: regex for predictable format
# Log format: 'ORDER id=ABC123 amount=49.99 status=shipped'
PATTERN = re.compile(r'ORDER id=(\w+) amount=([\d.]+) status=(\w+)')

def parse_order_log(log_line: str) -> OrderEvent:
    m = PATTERN.match(log_line)
    if not m:
        raise ValueError(f'Unexpected log format: {log_line!r}')
    return OrderEvent(
        order_id=m.group(1),
        amount=float(m.group(2)),
        status=m.group(3)
    )

# 0.01ms, deterministic, fully testable
event = parse_order_log('ORDER id=ABC123 amount=49.99 status=shipped')
Anti-Pattern 3: Agent as a Rule Engine

Business rules that can be expressed as if/else trees or configuration tables should not be delegated to an LLM. The LLM may produce the same answer most of the time, but 'most of the time' is not acceptable for business-critical logic.

from enum import Enum

class Tier(str, Enum):
    BRONZE = 'bronze'
    SILVER = 'silver'
    GOLD = 'gold'

# Business rule: discount by tier
DISCOUNT = {Tier.BRONZE: 0.0, Tier.SILVER: 0.05, Tier.GOLD: 0.10}

# BAD: ask an LLM for the discount
def get_discount_agent(tier: str) -> float:
    # Non-deterministic, slow, costly — wrong for a rules table
    ...

# GOOD: lookup table
def get_discount(tier: Tier) -> float:
    return DISCOUNT[tier]  # O(1), deterministic, easy to audit

# Adding a new tier means adding one line to the dict,
# not prompt-engineering a new LLM behaviour.
When Cost and Volume Rule Out Agents
Even when an agent could handle the task, the economics may rule it out. Consider a system that processes one million events per day:
Approach
Cost / million calls
Latency per call
Deterministic?
Python function
~$0.01 (compute only)
<1 ms
Yes
Traditional ML model
~$1 (GPU inference)
Yes
LLM agent (Haiku)
No
For a task like scoring one million events, the difference between a Python function and an LLM agent is
four to five orders of magnitude in cost
. This is not a premature optimisation — it is a fundamental architectural constraint.
A Practical Checklist Before Building an Agent
Run through these questions before committing to an agentic architecture:
Can I write a complete flowchart?
If yes, use a workflow engine or script.
Is my input always structured?
If yes, use schema validation and typed parsers.
Is my volume > 10k calls/day?
If yes, model LLM cost at your provider's pricing before committing.
Is latency < 200ms required?
If yes, agents cannot satisfy this without significant caching or fine-tuning.
Do regulators require deterministic outputs?
If yes, use a deterministic system or add a deterministic wrapper that verifies LLM output before acting.
Is the task a known pattern (ETL, CRUD, scheduling)?
If yes, use the established tool for that pattern.
When Agents Are Genuinely the Right Choice
After all the cautions above, agents do shine in a specific class of problems. Use an agent when:
The task requires
open-ended tool selection
— the agent must decide which of many possible tools to call based on the current state.
The input is
natural language or unstructured
and must be interpreted before any action is taken.
The execution path
cannot be enumerated in advance
— the agent must adapt based on intermediate results.
Volume and latency requirements
allow for agentic overhead
(e.g. a task that runs once per user request, not once per event).
The Hidden Costs of Agents
Beyond the per-call pricing, agents carry
operational costs
that only appear once you are in production:
Debugging is harder.

When a deterministic function returns the wrong answer, you trace the logic. When an agent returns the wrong answer, you must trace the LLM reasoning, tool calls, and intermediate state — often non-reproducibly.
Tests are flaky.

Unit tests for deterministic functions always pass or always fail. Agent tests can fail intermittently because the LLM occasionally makes different choices, requiring mocking or extensive golden-output testing.
Prompts require maintenance.
Model updates — even patch releases — can silently change agent behaviour. A prompt that worked with one model version may perform differently after an update.
Observability is more complex.
Tracing a multi-step agent loop requires structured logging of every message, tool call, and response. A function call requires only input/output logging.
Using Caching as a Middle Ground

If the task benefits from LLM reasoning but volume or cost is a concern, caching repeated LLM responses is often the right middle ground. Cache by a hash of the prompt and inputs so identical queries avoid a new call:

import hashlib, json
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

# In-process cache; for production use Redis with a TTL
_cache: dict = {}

def _prompt_key(system, user):
    payload = json.dumps({"system": system, "user": user}, sort_keys=True)
    return hashlib.sha256(payload.encode()).hexdigest()

def cached_classify(text, category_list):
    system = "Classify the text into exactly one of: " + ", ".join(category_list)
    key = _prompt_key(system, text)
    if key in _cache:
        return _cache[key]  # free, instant
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=50,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}]
    )
    result = response.content[0].text.strip()
    _cache[key] = result
    return result

# First call: LLM invoked
# Subsequent identical calls: instant, free
label = cached_classify("Cannot connect to database", ["bug", "feature", "question"])
Caching is appropriate only when the LLM output is
stable across identical inputs
— which is true for classification and extraction tasks but not for creative or conversational tasks.
Common Pitfalls When Teams Over-Agentify
The 'LLM as router' trap.
Using an LLM to decide which function to call when a dictionary lookup or if/else would work. This is only justified when routing logic itself requires language understanding.
Agent sprawl.
Teams that adopt agents for one use case begin adding agents everywhere. Establish a team-wide checklist that each new agent use case must pass.
Premature tool expansion.
Giving an agent many tools 'just in case' increases the probability of the agent choosing the wrong tool. Start with the minimum set of tools the task needs.
Ignoring the test harness cost.
A deterministic function needs one test file. An agent needs mocked LLM responses, recorded tool call sequences, and integration tests — a significantly larger investment.
The Migration Path: Start Simple, Add Agents Only When Needed
The best teams treat agents as a last resort after simpler approaches have been tried and measured. A three-stage migration path prevents over-engineering:
Stage 1: Rule-based.
Write the simplest possible logic. Measure its accuracy or coverage on real data. Many tasks are solved at this stage.
Stage 2: Enhanced rules.
Add more signals, keyword sets, or lightweight ML classifiers. This often gets you to 85% with much lower cost and full determinism.
Stage 3: Hybrid.
Use the rule-based system for high-confidence cases and delegate only the low-confidence tail to an LLM. This captures the accuracy gain at a fraction of the cost.

# Start simple: plain function
def categorise_ticket(title: str, body: str) -> str:
    title_lower = title.lower()
    if "crash" in title_lower or "error" in title_lower:
        return "bug"
    if "how" in title_lower or "?" in title_lower:
        return "question"
    return "feature"

# Works for 80% of tickets. Ship it.
category = categorise_ticket("App crashes on login", "Steps to reproduce...")

# --- 3 months later: accuracy is 72%, business wants 90% ---

# Step 2: fine-grained rules with more signals
KEYWORDS = {
    "bug": ["crash", "error", "broken", "fails", "exception", "traceback"],
    "question": ["how", "why", "can i", "is it possible", "docs"],
    "feature": ["add", "support", "implement", "allow", "enable"],
}

def categorise_v2(title: str, body: str) -> str:
    combined = (title + " " + body).lower()
    scores = {cat: sum(1 for kw in kws if kw in combined)
              for cat, kws in KEYWORDS.items()}
    return max(scores, key=scores.get)

# Reaches 85%. Cheaper and faster than an agent.

# --- Still not meeting the 90% target for edge cases ---

# Step 3: LLM agent only for low-confidence cases
from anthropic import Anthropic
client = Anthropic()

def categorise_hybrid(title: str, body: str) -> str:
    combined = (title + " " + body).lower()
    scores = {cat: sum(1 for kw in kws if kw in combined)
              for cat, kws in KEYWORDS.items()}
    top_score = max(scores.values())
    if top_score >= 2:  # high confidence: use fast rule
        return max(scores, key=scores.get)
    # low confidence: escalate to LLM
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=20,
        system="Reply with exactly one word: bug, question, or feature.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"{title}\n{body}"}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text.strip().lower()

# LLM invoked only for ~15% of tickets — cost drops 85%,
# accuracy meets the 90% target.
The hybrid approach is often the correct final architecture — not a pure agent. It gives the
accuracy benefits of an LLM
while keeping
cost, latency, and determinism
acceptable for the majority of inputs.
Evaluating Your Approach: Rule Engine vs. Agent
Before choosing an agent, build a small evaluation harness over a sample of real inputs. Compare the rule-based approach and the agent on accuracy and latency:
Running this evaluation before committing to an agent architecture takes one hour and can save months of operational pain. If the rule engine meets the accuracy target,
use the rule engine
Summary
Agents add latency, cost, and non-determinism — they must earn their place in the design.
Use scripts, rule engines, or workflow orchestrators when execution paths are known and inputs are structured.
At high volumes (>10k/day) or strict latency (<200ms), agents are usually not viable without caching or fine-tuning.
Agents shine for open-ended tasks: unstructured input, dynamic tool selection, unpredictable execution paths.
The checklist — Can I draw the flowchart? Is input structured? Is volume high? Is latency strict? — is a fast filter before any agent design.
